The lake will be going up a couple of feet a day possibly impacting the Wildlife area water depths as wellBE VERY CAREFULL ABOUT WHERE YOU ARE HUNTING AND THE POSSIBILITY OF THE WATER GETTING DEEPER.<br/><br/>Hunting conditions<br/><br/>Moderate<br/><br/>Expected hunting success<br/><br/>Poor<br/><br/>Comments<br/><br/>A permit/survey is required to hunt the Tuttle Creek Wildlife Area. 14th Rd south of the Black Vermillion Rd is closed due to flooding. Rolander is still closed at the bridge and the W.A. boat ramp parking areas areunder water again.<br/><br/>Birds numbers have been highly variable depending on day, time of day, and ice conditions. Ducks have not moved back into the area, 500 - 2,500 ducks. Mallards currently make up about 60% of the ducks on the area, rest of the birds are mixed species. Around 10,000 - 25,000 geese, mostly snows, with maybe a 1,000 darks. Almost all the birds are using the refuge at this time.<br/><br/>If the report has not been updated in a few days, please assume that conditions and bird numbers are the same as the last update.<br/><br/>Water level<br/><br/>Pool 1A is 59", 1B and 1C are around 46", 2 has about 36", 3A has 22", 3B has 24", 4A has 26", 4B has 25", 5 has 30".<br/><br/>Currently about 75% frozen with very thin ice.<br/><br/>Hunting conditions<br/><br/>Pool 2: Good moist soil production in areas that were sprayed the last couple years and in areas that were free of cattails. About 200 acres of cattails was aerially sprayed on 8/1/18.<br/><br/>Pool 3A: All of the pool was aerially sprayed for cattails on 8/1/18. Like always it will be closed to motorized boats.<br/><br/>Pool 3B: 209 acres were disked twice and drilled to millet. Another 40 acres was no-till drilled to millet. Another 300 acres of cattails have been disked. Good moist soil production.<br/><br/>Pool 4A: Some moist soil production.<br/><br/>Pool 4B: 80 acres were disked twice and planted to millet. Another 38 acres were no-till drilled to millet. Good moist soil production.<br/><br/>Pool 5: All 1,400 acres were burned and disked at least 5 times. There are a few areas of cattails along the dike and in some deeper spots that are still too wet to disk. Pool 5 will be open for teal season.<br/><br/>Burned an additional 1,500 acres of uplands and small wetlands in Pool 5 and 3B.<br/><br/>Use the foot crossings along the roads in all pools<br/><br/>Expected hunting success<br/><br/>Fair. Very few ducks or geese are being harvested at this time. Success is highly variable depending on the day.<br/><br/>iSportsman (electronic daily hunt permit system) will be required of all hunters this fall. THEY CAN ALSO VISIT THE CHEYENNE BOTTOMS WILDLIFE AREA PAGE ON iSPORTSMAN AT <br/><br/>Kingman State Fishing Lake is full. All marshes are planted to Japanese Millet and are full.<br/><br/>Hunting conditions<br/><br/>Good<br/><br/>WARNING-New rip rap has been added to the Kingman State Lake dam and until the metal has been removed, it poses a hazard to hunters, fishermen, and their dogs.<br/><br/>Expected hunting success<br/><br/>Fair to good, but improving with any cold fronts that would move birds down. Pressure is heavy and many animals are being hazed to the point that they are using private water.<br/><br/>Comments<br/><br/>Hunters! Bids closed for the NAWCA wetland project as of 10/17/2017. We started moving dirt the week of November 13, 2017 and the north project was completed last winter. We will be flooding those marshes west and north of the lake with a shallow depth in order to not threaten the integrity of the dikes while perennial vegetation is being established. Also, a new kayak/canoe launch has been installed on the dam just west of the refuge line to aid launching boats across the rip rap. We will be adding a hardened surface beside it to better facilitate launching. The south part of the NAWCA project in the old Bison Marsh was not started in 2018 due to wet conditions, however, it will be flooded and the work put off until 2019.<br/><br/>Approximately 3,200 ducks were observed on 12/7/18. Primarily mallard but some goldeneye, teal, and mergansers were also present. 200 Canada geese. Additional ducks may be present within flooded vegetation at the upper ends of the lake, but are not easily viewed. Scouting is encouraged prior to your hunt.<br/><br/>Lake approximately 90% ice covered on 12/10/18. With forecasted temperatures, anticipate ice conditions to change daily. Habitat conditions are slightly improved as compared to most years. Food producing vegetation became established on lake fringes as a result of drought conditions and declining lake levels. In mid-August rains returned and gradually filled the lake in early September. Flooding in October and November has diminished the quality of some of this vegetation however.<br/><br/>Approximately 1200 ducks and 800 Canada geese observed on 12/04. Various species of puddle ducks and divers. Flooded vegetation has made observation of ducks difficult. Scouting encouraged prior to your hunt.<br/><br/>Water level<br/><br/>Approximately 0.52' above conservation elevation (1339') on 12/04.<br/><br/>Hunting conditions<br/><br/>Most of the summer the lake was approximately 4.5' below conservation pool. Good moist soil production of vegetation has occurred along the exposed shore line and mud flat areas throughout the summer due to the low water levels. With a fall raise in water level improved habitat conditions may enhance opportunities for waterfowl hunters.<br/><br/>ATTENTION: i-Sportsman isREQUIRED to hunt this area. All hunters MUST use the electronic daily hunt permit system! Please check in, report your harvest, and check out using i-Sportsman for each and every hunt.<br/><br/>Ice cover in the marsh was still near 100% earlier in the week, but some of the ice has been thawing the last couple of days. Based on the weather forecast, which indicates less frigid temperatures over the next week, the amount of open water should gradually increase as we near the end of the first split of duck season.<br/><br/>Duck and goose numbers are changing daily as weather conditions and ice cover change. As of Friday morning, there were approximately 1,000 ducks and 15,000 - 18,000 geese in the Big Basin refuge. Goose numbers are highly variable throughout the day as the geese trade back and forth from feeding.<br/><br/>See "Water Levels" below for more information.<br/><br/>Remember that all activities in the refuge areas areprohibited, AND THE NORTH REFUGE UNIT IS NOW IN EFFECT, along with the south refuge in the Big Basin.<br/><br/>Current Information<br/><br/>As of Friday the 7th, road conditions are good.<br/><br/>Water levels<br/><br/>Big Basin:Most public hunting marshes are full.<br/><br/>Chain of Lakes: Most public hunting marshes are full.<br/><br/>Little Sinkhole Unit: Most public hunting marshes are full.<br/><br/>Hunting conditions<br/><br/>Conditions will hopefully be stable after the October flood event. There should be plenty of water for the rest of the season!<br/><br/>Expected hunting success<br/><br/>Fair. Please visit  to register prior to hunting season.<br/><br/>Kansas hunters should be aware that iSportsman Electronic Daily Hunt Permits are required on 25 wildlife areas, including McPherson Valley Wetlands (check the 2018 Kansas Hunting and Furharvesting Regulations Summary or kdwpt.isportsman.net for a list).<br/><br/>iSportsman replaces the old paper permits and allows hunters to conveniently check in and out using a telephone, smartphone or home computer. As with the old paper system, information gathered helps area managers tailor management practices specific to an area and hunter preference. The paper system was inconvenient for hunters and labor intensive for managers.<br/><br/>**THERE IS A "NO TRASH POLICY" IN EFFECT AT MCPHERSON VALLEY WETLANDS. Some of the boat ramps are still closed.<br/><br/>There has been very good moist soil production on the shoreline and mudflats (yellow nut sedge, smartweed, barnyard grass), and this vegetation is starting to see significant flooding now that the water levels have started to rise. Crops in the areas surrounding the reservoir have been poor due to drought this year.<br/><br/>5% Ice coverage.<br/><br/>Expected hunting success<br/><br/>Fair.<br/><br/>Comments<br/><br/>Remember Marion Reservoir has ZEBRA MUSSELS. Make sure to Clean, Drain and Dry your equipment. Clean all visible vegetation and mud from decoys, bags, waders, etc. Drain water from decoy bags and boats. Allow equipment to Dry for a five day period before using it at another area, OR wash with high-pressure hot water. Many of the roads around the reservoir can get very greasy after rain, or melting snow, please use good judgement about which roads you try to travel on.<br/><br/>Small numbers of mallards, mixed duck species, and Canada geese were observed using the reservoir this weekAround 5,000 ducks have been observed around the lake and on the wetlands. The majority of the ducks observed were mallards. Around 10,000 snow geese are also using the reservoir.<br/><br/>Water level<br/><br/>The COE has been slowly dropping the reservoir water level over the past couple of weeks after a foot and a half rise. The water is currently a half foot above conservation pool but will continue to drop until they close the gates. GOOD WATER AMOUNTS IN WETLANDS.<br/><br/>Hunting conditions<br/><br/>GOOD VEGETATION EXCEPT WHERE REPEATED FLOODING HAS TAKEN A TOLL.<br/><br/>Expected hunting success<br/><br/>Fair to Good.<br/><br/>Comment<br/><br/>For updated waterfowl numbers and weather conditions call (620) 331-6820. Individuals utilizing the wildlife area need to be aware of the refuge boundaries. Maps can be obtained at the Fisheries & Wildlife office (below the dam) or at the State Park (on the lake side).<br/><br/>Small numbers of mallards and mixed duck species were observed using the reservoir this week. A few hundred Canada geese were seen using local ponds and harvested crop fieldsReservoir is still ice free, but expect it to start closing up in near future. Boats ramps are all open and accessible as of 12/07/18. Big and little marshes below dam are pretty much iced up on edges. Birds are scattered around lake, but concentrations can be found at major creek entrances, such as mouth of Otter Creek.<br/><br/>Lower marsh is currently 100% full, and consist of some millet and moist soil annuals. Upper Marsh below dam is 100% full. All department managed marshes are open for public waterfowl hunting, and are located below John Redmond Dam south of spillway. Beware, both the upper and lower marsh's contain some deep water in lower portions, well over 6 feet in depth. The large sedimentation basins located below damn are not open to public hunting and are under private ownership.<br/><br/>Expected hunting success<br/><br/>Average. If you have boat and are able to access remote areas of reservoir, especially timbered creeks out of the wind, I would expect to have above average success.<br/><br/>To see real time John Redmond elevations please visit---- <br/><br/>Comments<br/><br/>For information on Flint Hills National Wildlife Area and the marshes on the upper end of the lake please contact FHNWR @ 620-392-5553.<br/><br/>All hunters must check-in daily using iSportsman electronic check-in from a PC, smartphone, or by phone before hunting and check out at the end of the hunt regardless of harvest. We now have a zero tolerance policy on failure to comply with iSportsman and check station requirements. Please remember to fill out the check-out report Go to:<br/><br/>Outboard motors are restricted on this wildlife area and are allowed in Unit G and in the boat lane only in Unit A-East. Outboards are not allowed in any other wetland units. Electric trolling motors are unrestricted. Boats must be operated at wakeless speed. Boats must have sufficient PFDs for occupants.<br/><br/>Go to MDCWA and click the "News" icon near the top for more information on general waterfowl habitat. Waterfowl report is updated only if there are significant changes in waterfowl numbers or hunting conditions.<br/><br/>Waterfowl counts given on this report are based on what was observed on the day the report is updated. Waterfowl numbers vary greatly from day to day and weather conditions and hunting pressure will affect the number of waterfowl on the area.<br/><br/>Mallard Migration Rank = 1 (0 = no mallards and 10 = peak migration)<br/><br/>11/21/2018 ( 11:00 AM )---<br/><br/>3 Duck Marsh- Both units are pumped and full of water, 70 mallards.<br/><br/>Willow Marsh- North and South units are pumped and full. No waterfowl were observed at the time of the survey.<br/><br/>Sundance Marsh- No waterfowl were observed at the time of survey.<br/><br/>Lowman's Cove- Full of water.<br/><br/>Shoveler Slough- LOCATION: South parking lot for Shoveler Slough is at the intersection of W 317th St. & S Davies Rd. 100 mallards and 20 unknown ducks were observed at the time of this survey. (NOTE: Motorized boats are prohibited)<br/><br/>Refuge- Observed waterfowl at the time of the survey 150 mixed divers, largely Goldeneye. Along with approximately 75 Canada geese, 30 gadwall, and 25 mallards.<br/><br/>Japanese and German millet (50/50 mix) has been drilled in all 5 of our wetlands and is limited due to drought conditions throughout the summer. Millet as well as Milo was planted in alternating strips throughout all of our wetlands to provide varying structure heights as well as options for ducks to feed on moist soil vegetation and grains. Varying food sources an abundance of invertebrates should provide enough food for the ducks to hang around all season.<br/><br/>Expected hunting success<br/><br/>Fair<br/><br/>Comments<br/><br/>All hunters utilizing Melvern Wildlife Area are required to obtain a free daily hunt permit through the new electronic permit system. Hunters can register now to begin using the electronic system at  For more information(click here). Waterfowl hunters utilizing the main lake are only required to obtain a permit when hunting west of Hoch RoadSouth Unit- Dry and will remain dry for 2018-2019 seasons for renovation work (see "Area News").<br/><br/>For more information on the water level conditions for upcoming season please see the area news by clicking link below and clicking on the "NEWS" icon.<br/><br/>Hunting conditions<br/><br/>Good.<br/><br/>Pools are all full enough now to run both outboards and mud motors throughout pools 1, 2A and 2B. There are no motor restrictions on the area just be courteous to other hunters as you transverse through hunting pools.<br/><br/>This weeks weather looks like we could potentially freeze up or at least have >1/2 inch ice towards the weekend, so please use extreme caution as weather and conditions change.<br/><br/>Corn and millet production was exceptional this year. Moist soil seed production was some of the best we have seen on the area and we are extremely pleased. Habitat and water levels are excellent for this time of year. Please keep in mind that it is a long season and we will continually be staging water levels up throughout the season providing more food with each stage up.<br/><br/>Please see the area news page for a breakdown of habitat types within each pool.<br/><br/>Expected hunting success<br/><br/>Southeast Zone Regular Duck Season November 10, 2018- January 6, 2019 and January 12-27, 2018<br/><br/>Fair<br/><br/>Hunting continues to be fair. There will be ice developing later in the week so utilize extreme caution with changing weather conditions. Hunters have been averaging around 2 birds per hunter this season.<br/><br/>We still have hunters that have not checked out of the iSportsman system. Please check to make sure it is not you and please remember to check out by 11:59 daily. Please visit  each individual hunter needs to check in/out and report his/her harvest totals. i.e., NOT A GROUP HARVEST TOTAL.<br/><br/>Waterfowl hunting in Pool 8 is restricted to youth hunters and their mentor on weekends and holidays. | Tá craobhachandoile i Kansas ó 1993 agus sa Lár-Clayy ó 1961 ceadaithe ag an tSeirbhís Iasc SAM agus Fiadhúlra i naoi dtír sa 10 stát i Lár na Gaillimhe. Cé go gcuireann daoine áirithe fiach crúine i gcoinne fiach crúna, déantar fiach craenach ar bhonn inbhuanaithe faoi phlean bainistíochta daonra atá ceadaithe ag an tSeirbhís Iasc SAM agus Fiadhúlra. Tá údar le fiach spóirt freisin chun dáileadh réasúnach crainn gainimh a chothabháil.Má éiríonn tiúchan ró-ard i roinnt réimsí, is féidir le luascúlacht barr tarlú agus riosca galair a mhéadú i gcás crainn. Tá fiach Crane thar a bheith dúshlánach toisc go bhfuil craenacha an-caoin agus meastar go bhfuil feoil Craein táille tábla den scoth, is dócha an chuid is fearr de na héin cluiche imirceacha.<br/><br/>Tá an daonra seo ar cheann de naoi gcinn i Meiriceá Thuaidh agus tá thart ar 500,000 éan ar an taobh istigh den chreatas agus tá sé i bhfad níos mó le fáil trí Kansas agus an gheimhridh anseo. An líon is mó daonra eile faoi 20-25,000 éan.<br/><br/>Rinneadh rialacháin Fiach a chur le chéile agus pleananna teagmhais a úsáidtear chun an seans go mbeidh craenacha á n-úsáid trí thimpiste ag sealgairí craenóideacha. Cé go dtosaíonn craenacha gainimh ag díriú ar uimhreacha i shuaimhneach le linn Dheireadh Fómhair go déanach, cuireadh moill ar thús an tséasúir seilge go dtí an Chéadaoin an chéad Satharn i mí na Samhna chun ligean don chuid is mó de na craenacha a bhfuil súil leo dul ar imirce tríd an stát. Tosaíonn uaireanta lámhaigh do craenacha gainimh ag éirí níos gréine seachas uair an chloig leath sula n-éireoidh siad gréine chun seans a fháil go mbeidh siad ag lámhach craenach ar choirnéal an dromlaigh mar gheall ar dhálaí solais íseal. (Is é Kansas an t-aon stát sa Lár-Mhómhar chun moill a chur ar uaireanta lámhach chun craenacha a chosaint.) Soláthraíonn an stát cónaidhme, Turgann Náisiúnta Fiadhúlra Quivira, agus Cheyenne Bottoms Education pleananna teagmhais Limistéar Fiadhúlra maidir le cad iad na gníomhaíochtaí a dhéanamh má tá crainn a thiomáint i láthair le linn séasúir fiaigh.<br/><br/>Cén fáth Crane - grianghraf ag Mike Blair, KDWP<br/><br/>Ní mór do charn gainimh agus sealgairí uisce a bheith ar an eolas go bhfuil a dríodair craenacha ag tarlú níos minice i Kansas, go háirithe i n-áiteanna Cheyenne agus i limistéir Quinira, agus a bheith in ann a aithint cé acu craenacha a thiomáint. Cé a bhfuil craenacha agus craenacha gainimh cosúil le méid agus cruth, ach go bhfuil craenacha á n-úsáid bán le leideanna sciatháin dubha.Is fíneáil de suas le $ 100,000 é an pionós le haghaidh craein a lámhach, agus/nó suas le bliain amháin sa phríosún.<br/><br/>Tástáil ar líne le haghaidh Hunters Craein Sandhill - Tá an tástáil éigeantach do gach sealgairí Craein salla.Is é an tástáil 378k i méid agus beidh a ghlacadh cúpla nóiméad a íoslódáil má tá tú móideim. Mura bhfuil do chuid féin, iarrfar ort Flash a íoslódáil 6.<br/><br/>Whooping Crane Brócúr - Eolas ar cénaí agus conas idirdhealú a dhéanamh orthu ó speicis a bhfuil cuma cosúil leo.<br/><br/>WS Information for Waterfowl agus Sandhill Crane Hunters - Is é an cuspóir an suíomh gréasáin seo a chur ar fáil d'fhóin uisce agus sealgairí gaineamhill Craein faisnéis a laghdú ar an dóchúlacht go mbeidh lámhach neamhdhleathach ag éin imirceacha a d'fhéadfadh cuma craenacha gainimh, ach nach bhfuil aon séasúr oscailte agus go bhfuil siad cosanta ag sealgairí éanlaithe talún Chónaidhme 16 agus níos sine caithfidh Stampáil agus Caomhnú Bird fiach (Stampal Waterfal fenderal Uisceshaoth) agus ní mór do gach sealgairí atá ag teastáil a fháil ceadúnas fiach freisin ina leith Stamp Uisceadán Stáit Kansas agus a bheith ina stampa faisnéis (HIP) roimh fiach lacha, geese, nó mengers. (Siad siúd nach bhfuil ag teastáil chun ceadúnas fiaigh Kansas san áireamh ar dhaoine fiach a gcuid talún féin, agus cónaitheoirí 15 agus níos óige)<br/><br/>NUA DO 2016: Níl an ceadúnas fiaigh Uisceadán 48 uair an chloig ar fáil a thuilleadh do sealgairí eas flip, tá sé in ionad an Ceadúnas Hunt Bliantúil.<br/><br/>Stampaí agus Ceadúnas Uisce - Is féidir gach stampa agus ceadúnas uisce-thiomáint a cheannach in aon Gníomhaire ceadúnaithe, Oifig Oibríochtaí Prat, nó ar líne, EXCEPT don Stampa Ferderawow a cheannaítear in Oifig Phoist na Stát Aontaithe, Oifigí Réigiúnacha agus Páirce, agus is féidir gníomhairí ceadúnais a roghnú. Tá gach stampa uiscechoill go maith tríd an séasúr.<br/><br/>Stampa Kash HIP: $2.50<br/><br/>Stampa Uisce: $ 10<br/><br/>Stampa Fuite: $ 22.50 - Arna cheannach in aon oifig KDWP nó in aon Oifig Poist de chuid na Stát Aontaithe.<br/><br/>Má tá fiach Tomhla Crane, chomh maith le ceadúnas fiach (más gá), cead fiaigh gaineamh cónaidhme arna eisiúint tríd agus tá sé bailíochtaithe ag an Roinn Hans Fiadhúlra agus Páirceanna is gá chun craenacha fiach. Níl na ceadanna Crane a fhaightear i stáit eile bailí i Kansas. Tá an cead bailíochtaithe (táille bailíochtú) ar fáil ag aon ghníomhaire ceadúnaithe, ar líne, Oifig Oibríochtaí Prat, nó oifig Réigiúnach.<br/><br/>Ní mór do gach sealgairí Craein gainimh pas a fháil ar an "Tástáil le haghaidh Hunters Crane Sandhill" sula bhfaigheann siad cead fiaigh inneall.<br/><br/>"Tástáil ar líne le haghaidh Hunters Crane Sandhill". Cliceáil ar an nasc seo a chur ar thástáil ar líne a sholáthraíonn eolas faoi conas crainn a aithint, craenacha gainimh, agus speicis cosúil le breathnú, chomh maith le cumas an hunter a roghnú shots sábháilte. Tá an tástáil siamsaíocht agus faisnéiseach agus ní thógann sé ach cúpla nóiméad.<br/><br/>Tá an t-igearchló Sebelius gar don airde céanna le bliain anuas. Tá an fhaisnéis luachmhar mar sin le do thoil a choinneáil ag iarraidh a sheiceáil-i, fiú tar éis an fiach.Ní mian linn saincheisteanna teicniúla chun cosc a chur ar dhuine ó fiach. Má tá an córas cárta á úsáid agat, ní mór an chuid is fearr den chead dhá chuid a líonadh amach agus a chur ar ais chuig an stáisiún suirbhé roimh an fiach agus an chuid bun a choinneáil leis an Hunter chun gníomhaíocht fiaigh thaifeadadh. Tar éis an fiach, cuirfear an chuid bun ar ais freisin chuig an stáisiún suirbhé áit a mbaileofar na ceadanna agus a thaifeadtar chun cabhrú le bainisteoirí an limistéar a fheabhsú. Cuidíonn an fhaisnéis thábhachtach seo a bhailítear ó cheadanna an fhiach le bainisteoirí cleachtais bainistíochta a bhaineann go sonrach le limistéar agus le rogha an seal.Gabhaimid buíochas leat as do foighne.<br/><br/>PÉID DO CHÉID CUILE GÍORTHA, DO CHUIDEACHTA nó AN ARD-CHÚ DO CHUID IS GACH DUINEANN AN GACH DUINEÚ GACH NA DUIRÍOCHTA. Níos mó lachain agus géanna ag teacht isteach ar aghaidheanna aimsire.<br/><br/>Céim 1 (= Is measa 10 = Is fearr)<br/><br/>leibhéal an uisce<br/><br/>Ag leibhéal caomhnaithe.<br/><br/>Coinníollacha Fiach<br/><br/>Insaidí in 2018 tógadh an taiscumar go dtí 7 gcosanna os cionn leibhéal caomhantais.Tá na rudaí seo a leanas mar thoradh ar an taiscumar ag an leibhéal seo agus ansin scaoileadh uisciúcháin a bheith ag an leibhéal caomhantais faoi láthair. Mar gheall ar an mbruach ag fás go mall agus os cionn na meán báistí, tá go leor fás ar an gcósta air le limistéir theoranta de mhodláin lom nochta. D'fhéadfadh an fásra feadh an chósta a bheith tairbheach do sealgairí lacha ach laghdaíonn an méid limistéir atá ag sileadh le haghaidh géanna. Gheobhaidh sealgairí Bád go leor réimsí d'adhmad tuile. Beidh Hunters in ann logáil isteach trí ríomhaire, smartphone, nó glaoch i ó fón póca nó ar líne fón gach lá roimh fhiach cead laethúil, agus tuairisc a thabhairt ar ais cosúil leis na ceadanna laethúla páipéar. Ní mór do gach sealgaire aonair a bheith le seiceáil isteach agus a talbhaí fómhar/a thuairisciú, i.e., NÍ GHLACFAR AR AN EOLAS A GHLAIST IOMLÁN.<br/><br/>Tá an fiach le sruth i linn Snámha 8 teoranta do sealgairí óige agus a meantóir ar an deireadh seachtaine agus laethanta saoire. |

Make friends/Surround yourself with smart people.: Not extremely practical unless you are a famous VC but even then not trivial.<br/><br/>I think it's quite practical to surround yourself with smart people without being a famous VC. I've done it. it might even be easier without being famous, unless by smart, you mean "Smart, tall and well-spoken" or "Smart, tall, and able to hold the right kind of conflicting ideas in your head without emotional distress" - which is often what VC seem to mean by smart. I mean, don't get me wrong, a lot of the ycombinater kids I've met really seemed pretty smart, but they also had the social bits you need to be good business people, and if you demand those social bits along with the 'smart' - and you should, if you are looking for business people and not just technical people - those people become dramatically more difficult to find, and when you do find them, they're difficult to be friends with, as you said, unless you are very high status, because they are very high status.<br/><br/>If you just want someone with a high G, though, and are okay with people who are short or shy or fat or who have emotional issues, as you should be if you are looking for people who can become good technical people, G alone does not confer much by way of status in this society. I've hired more than one person who tests two standard deviations better than I do by that measure. hell I've dated a few people who test two standard deviations better than I do.<br/><br/>Now, PG, I imagine, when talking about making investment decisions, probably is talking about "vc smart" - people who are smart, but also good looking, who have good attitudes and who have the other business person attributes, so in the context of this article, you're probably right. but I'm just saying, smart people usually aren't high status people. It's not hard to get smart friends, if you are willing to skimp on some of the status-granting attributes.<br/><br/>What makes you think height is so important? I have not noticed any correlation. One of the nice things about startups vs politics is that appearance is much less important for success. It is us who need to do a bit of the insight gathering now.<br/><br/>Coincidentally went to an effectual entrepreneurship workshop by Prof. Sarasvathy y'day.<br/><br/>Her learnings from talking to 'expert entrepreneurs' are that<br/><br/>1. they do not believe the future can be predicted, esp in the early stages of a new venture<br/><br/>2. they prefer to create the future in areas with high uncertainty by forging partnerships with people they know already, and who are also investing in the same areas<br/><br/>3. they go to the customer at a very low 'acceptable loss' as they can, building as little of the product as they can, investing as little as they can, and getting the first initial set of customers as partners.<br/><br/>4. they jump in to a fast iteration of execution/sales that helps them discover/create the market, relying on sales as market research.<br/><br/>5. they find pleasant surprises along the way that shape their execution/market/venture and they change to fit the new reality - and they shape that reality along the way<br/><br/>Almost certainly elderly scientists in particular; if he meant a general statement then the juxtaposition with the previous sentence is very odd. Your strength being 'always right' is a strength, but relying on it without proof is a weakness. The scientific method delivers an expert to their position, but it also keeps one in their place.<br/><br/>We always need to show our work. The fact that an expert can crutch on the Markovian Weather doesn't mean I should trust them in any way based on their word. Trust me, says the expert, I am always right. There are those who are right and there are those who are right with proof.<br/><br/>I am noticing a trend in Paul's essays. The last two were about changing paradigms. The first was about how a second read of a book leads to a different understanding, and this essay is about not letting your previous beliefs close your mind about things that can change.<br/><br/>I like though how he circles back to two fundamentals. One, human nature doesn't really change, and Two, betting on people is a better indicator of the future than betting on ideas.<br/><br/>Probably the biggest mistake people make is looking for confirming evidence instead of using evidence to determine the relative likelihood of competing hypotheses.<br/><br/>Even a large body of confirming evidence doesn't prove anything because that same evidence could be consistent with other hypotheses you never considered. You need to evaluate the diagnosticity of that evidence. (i.e. a patients high fever has almost no diagnostic value in determining what is wrong, because so many illnesses are consistent with that evidence.<br/><br/>Whereas, one shred of evidence can disprove a hypothesis completely. Which is why good doctors rule out things to identify the most likely problem.<br/><br/>These ideas PG is talking about are, IMO, some of the most important things for founders to understand.<br/><br/>Don't look to confirm your theories, look to disprove them. Don't accept lore, challenge everything. Most importantly though, always evaluate competing hypothesis to prevent you from just trying to confirm what you are looking for.<br/><br/>I use this framework, though learnt it by reading Fenmayn's writings, when there's a debate as to use tool A or tool B to get X. And it's been very effective so far. I have a vague sense that B is better and a colleague is particular about A. Earlier I used to look deeper and deeper into how B is good at doing X. But realized that I wasn't convincing enough. Then I remembered Feynman's saying to the effect "the best way to prove something is to try very hard to disprove it". This has helped quite a lot in coming up with objective comparisons and use right tool for the task at hand; or in general to come up with the right solution the problem at hand.<br/><br/>@pmarca has an interesting thread on this on Twitter [1]. In Peter Thiel's terminology [2], PG's view is optimistic nondeterminate, which might be fine for a VC but might not be the best choice for a founder.<br/><br/>Unfortunately Thiel's views don't really map to a "hard" 2x2 matrix--the axes should be considered to represent spectra or gradients instead. (I'm not presuming to be smarter than Thiel here, just extrapolating rationally... nobody believes the future to be entirely determinate or entirely indeterminate.)<br/><br/>In light of that, I suspect PG and Thiel actually live in the same quadrant of determinate optimism. I haven't read PG suggest that we should just "wait" for the future to happen--he's merely suggesting that the most common path toward building the future is on a wave. (And Thiel built PayPal that way--he recognized a version of the future that was on the cusp of being realized and took it upon himself to make it happen.)<br/><br/>Kahneman attributes a lot to 'luck' of what experts achieve especially when it comes to predicting something. I am a bit surprised that PG does not give 'shear luck' its fair share.<br/><br/>I am assuming that many (most?) of the startup founders, when they are being interviewed by the potential investors, are 'complete strangers'. By focusing objectively on ideas than subjectively or intuitively on people doing them, they believe that they mitigate the so-called risk. I am not convinced that by judging people rather than by judging ideas (in supposedly short amount of time) the chances of succeeding go up, because if we are often wrong judging ideas, what makes us good at judging people who are ever changing too?<br/><br/>Of course, there is an undeniable 'credit history' part wherein if a 'successful' founder comes back with another idea, many investors are ready to 'shower money' on her/him -- I don't find anything majorly wrong with that attitude, but that alone does not guarantee success, I believe.<br/><br/>That may be one of the very few context where judging people is easier than ideas.<br/><br/>Ideas that grow huge look completely random, except when you happen to know the one or two details that make them promising. And you can never know if you know all of those details.<br/><br/>Almost all people that make something big share some few treats that most people lack, what makes this a useful filter. Of course people can change, what reduces the effectiveness of the filter, but it's still better than random.<br/><br/>I work with making technology teams deliver faster. It's nothing as complex and tricky as what Paul's doing, but I also get to see lots of teams and make correlations between people and performance.<br/><br/>One of the things I've noticed is that over a long period of time, the more arrogant and certain team members are, the less likely they are to deliver. Folks that are always learning have a tendency to kick ass. Not so much with folks who think they've already arrived. I read in an article on HN a few weeks back that Google calls this "intellectual humility" and looks for it as a hiring trait.<br/><br/>One of the essential tensions of being a software developer is the fact that software isn't really useful on its own. It has to do something. That thing it's doing is the domain. Whatever very-specific information that the software system is organizing and passing, more specifically.<br/><br/>For example, I maintain an e-commerce system. More specifically, I maintain an e-commerce system built for a cosmetics company. E-commerce is a field, cosmetics is a field, cosmetics e-commerce is specifically the domain I need to become an expert in. The more I can differentiate cosmetics e-commerce from other kinds of e-commerce, the more well-tailored I can make my app. And the more well-tailored I can make this app, the better I'll be able to understand the more general case of making custom e-commerce systems for other industries.<br/><br/>The more I go through this process of understanding the specific needs of the industry, the more domain expertise I'm building. Domains can subdivide as much as industries subdivide. Say the cosmetic industry started to split along product lines, then I might start gaining insight into how lipstick companies operate, and be able to tailor a product offering straight to them.<br/><br/>The domain doesn't have to mean commercial industry. You might have domain expertise, say, in XML parsing. Then you could wax poetic on the differences between Nokogiri and say, Chrome's internal parser.<br/><br/>So, essentially, it just means "expertise". More specifically, the type of expertise that allows you to customize a software system around it. Which can be in practice quite different from normal expertise. The guy who devises our makeup formulas is definitely an expert.<br/><br/>But he can't design the system to actually manufacture it. The guy who writes those systems might not understand the actual formulas, so the two types of experts have to work together.<br/><br/>When startups talk about the need to be a domain expert, it means getting out of just pure programming and into the actual meat. Rather than hire an expert, to become one yourself. This not only allows you to reduce staff, it also makes it easier to make better, more custom solutions. The Soylent guys have to make the system to manufacture their product, but they also must understand nutrition, food, the regulatory environment of the food market.<br/><br/>I really feel this. As a founder, my biggest strength is that I'm a domain expert. Right now, I'm doing coding because I don't have money to hire someone else to do it, and I'm perfectly capable of it. But I feel it's not a good use of my time. Any decent programmer could do what I do most of the time right now. What they can't do is understand the domain as intimately as I do.<br/><br/>So as soon as there's money to hire programmers, I'm getting out of the coding biz and hiring others to do it. I want to focus on working with my customers, understanding their needs better than I already do, and guiding programmers to build for those needs.<br/><br/>This not only allows you to reduce staff, it also makes it easier to make better, more custom solutions.<br/><br/>I would even go as far to argue some level of domain expertise is almost required to make solutions. Even with perfect requirements (oxymoron I know), a programmer still has to make a ton of tiny domain driven decisions while writing a piece of software.<br/><br/>As you mentioned, software on its own isn't very useful. A programmer who learns even general business is much more useful.<br/><br/>I really dig this post. As it happens, the blending of domain expertise and software skills is a big reason I moved into devops. I wanted to make my domain expertise as portable as I could, while relying heavily on my software skills. Your cosmetics e-commerce app scales according to the same principles as most others', using the same techniques. Which is cool, because it increases the amount of useful knowledge in my head and allows me to apply it to a lot of different problems. (I call my role "being a multiplier", and it's a lot of fun.)<br/><br/>A set of disciplines, or majors, other than computer-science (or alternatively something other than core programming/software-engineering skills). The word is typically used in CS/sofware-engineering circles.<br/><br/>An (arguably crude) example:<br/><br/>Think of who designed an mp3 encoder/decoder? A programmer? Does becoming a good programmer and/or getting a typical 4 year CS degree, sufficient to come up with an audio encoder/decoder?<br/><br/>No. You have to understand audio signals, their properties, learn how to analyze them, etc. That requires knowledge from the disciplines of physics, and electrical engineering (e.g., digital signal processing), maybe even some biology of sound. And if you learn that, you become a part-programmer, part-acoustic-engineer. That "acoustic-engineer" bit is your "domain expertise", i.e., something you need to know beyond CS to get your work done.<br/><br/>You might say, "why do I have be an acoustic engineer? why can't I, as a programmer, work with another guy, who is not a programmer but an expert acoustic engineer?". Yes you can. In that case, you're being the software guy, and he's being the domain-expert.<br/><br/>Sometimes collaboration between a software-engineer and a domain-expert works. Sometimes it helps if the software-engineer himself develops domain-expertise (so one guy playing two roles).<br/><br/>Domain expertise is not limited to one domain only. It could be no-domain (a pure CS programming, like database, web-development etc, although you could call them domains within CS), or a domain consisting of multiple disciplines (audio-processing comprising physics, DSP), or multiple domains (acoustic-engineering plus submarine-engineering, for example), and so on.<br/><br/>There is an additional attribute that goes a long way in staying an expert in a changing world - humility or acknowledging that you do not or cannot know the future. PG hints at this when he says.. "...admit you have no idea what the right direction is, and try instead to be super sensitive to the winds of change...". It is also paraphrased quite well in the maxim of "Stay hungry, stay foolish" popularized by Jobs.<br/><br/>But humility that stems from ignorance is at odds with popular expectations of an expert. The problem is what worked in the past may not work in the future. Being sensitive to changes in the world around you, requires you to momentarily stop being an expert and look at the world from the eyes of a novice or beginner. This is not only hard but sometimes impossible to do because of how we change as we acquire new knowledge. [2]<br/><br/>So the trick is to be somewhat schizophrenic - being able to simultaneously view the world as an expert with the fresh thinking of a beginner or a novice.<br/><br/>This assumes that the goal is to be Jobs, and I completely agree with the assessment that you need to be schizophrenic. I would like to point out, the expectation is to be detail oriented(execution/expert) while leading a larger org macro goal (learning/strategy). In the right contexts with appropriate resources, this can & does magnify personalities and outputs!<br/><br/>> So the trick is to be somewhat schizophrenic - being able to simultaneously view the world as an expert with the fresh thinking of a beginner or a novice.<br/><br/>You already mentioned this, but this is extremely at odds with how experts are valued, especially by non-experts. People want answers -- not necessarily painful truths -- and ostensibly that's what they pay for. "What do you mean you're not sure? You're supposed to be an expert!"<br/><br/>This trick might work when you're a Jobs figure (or a Paul Graham, or a YC company) and you don't have non-experts to answer to. For everyone else, staying hungry and foolish means you'll be seen as hungry and foolish, and the market values you as a hungry fool, not an expert.<br/><br/>> There is an additional attribute that goes a long way in staying an expert in a changing world - humility or acknowledging that you do not or cannot know the future.<br/><br/>Sadly there's one rather important setting where this does not work at all :<br/><br/>Interviewing with a non-technical interviewer. Like one has to do for pretty much any non-individual contributor job at all but the very largest companies.<br/><br/>I keep having this impulse and I find it actually convinces people you don't know what you're talking about. The more algorithms you've seen fail spectacularly at prediction, the more capable you are (especially if you figured out why), but the less capable you appear.<br/><br/>Furthermore people always believe they can do better by just looking at the data and going with their gut feeling. This is not true, I've only seen people do that in a few cases. And I've seen people screw it up royally in more than a few cases. I think the successes were lucky guesses, because while they looked like they knew what they were doing, I could never get a decent response to the simple question : your arguments work just as well for 5 other predictions one might make, so why this one ? So I'm forced to doubt there. Either they knew more than they were willing to admit (one was also a political figure, so ...), or they were lucky guesses.<br/><br/>And yet the truly successful people have gotten to where they are by sticking to their beliefs:<br/><br/>- Warren Buffet has always believed that companies should be valued based on their profits, not their share price performance. Thus he didn't invest in the tech bubble of the 90s, and was forced to under-perform the market significantly for many years whilst everyone thought he was getting too old for the new way of things. Yet he stuck to his beliefs, and it turned out that he was right - the Nasdaq bubble evaporated and he again out-performed other funds in the long term.<br/><br/>- Steve Jobs always stuck to his belief that computing devices for consumers should be fully integrated and beautifully designed. After getting kicked out of Apple and loosing the OS war to MS, he could have easily told himself his approach was wrong. But whether he is right or wrong became irrelevant - his belief was so powerful that it was contagious.<br/><br/>- Zuck, Brin, and others have succeeded because they stuck to their beliefs instead of selling out early on.<br/><br/>- Darwin stuck to his unorthodox beliefs until his theories became accepted.<br/><br/>All of these people have/had a belief - a model of the universe which they passionately believed in. Having such a model means that predicting the future becomes possible, even though one can't predict exactly when that future will come, the same way Warren Buffet will never tell you what the market will do tomorrow, yet he certainly has a belief in where things are going long term. Jobs was too early with the Newton, yet his continued belief caused him to strike gold later on with the iPhone.<br/><br/>There is an interesting difference between fundamental beliefs and "expert knowledge". The later constantly needs to adapt to take new information into account, but it is our fundamental beliefs that determine how we interpret that expert knowledge.<br/><br/>For what it's worth, my startup investment belief has always been to: invest in companies that are run by great people that have created the best product in a growing market that has profitable competitors. I'll leave it up to the "experts" to speculate on which markets will grow most, or which competitors will win out. If Warren Buffet can't do it, I won't bother either. All that we can really judge to the best of our ability is the quality of the product and the team.<br/><br/>> And yet the truly successful people have gotten to where they are by sticking to their beliefs<br/><br/>There are a great many "startup mantras" which seem contradictory at a glance:<br/><br/>1. Founders should insist on product excellence, but release early (be embarrassed by the first version).<br/><br/>2. Startups only die when their founders give up, but founders who don't cut their losses at the right time lose credibility.<br/><br/>3. [The one at hand...] The best founders are driven by a secret about the world that other people don't know yet, and yet good founders are never married to an idea.<br/><br/>None of those things are actually contradictory, though:<br/><br/>1. An MVP is not a shitty product, it is a minimal product (founders should be embarrassed by lack of features, but the ones that are there should work well).<br/><br/>2. Both statements are true, even if uncomfortable. Add to the list of difficult balances founders must achieve.<br/><br/>3. Both statements are true here, too. I suspect that the best founders are actually driven by a family of secrets ("hypotheses" in the essay at hand) and yet would not think twice about adapting them in the face of new evidence. (Your example of Darwin is a good one to consider here--he stuck to his beliefs because he had strong evidence for them, and he would have changed his beliefs had new evidence presented itself. The prevailing opinion of society did not factor into that process.)<br/><br/>I think PG's point conflates a whole bunch of unrelated stuff, including:<br/><br/>1. Experts as people with unusual technical competence in one or more related fields.<br/><br/>2. Experts as 'big picture' prophets of the future.<br/><br/>3. Experts as 'small picture' refiners of future tech.<br/><br/>4. Experts as packagers, innovators, and marketers who create new markets for the work of 2 and 3.<br/><br/>5. Experts as investors who look for all of the above and try to work out if they can make money from them.<br/><br/>Aren't these mostly unrelated job descriptions? There's no reason why a brilliant academic who understands compiler design would have anything useful to say about the future of consumer electronics.<br/><br/>Likewise, I wouldn't have expected Steve Jobs to contribute much to (say) research into room temperature semiconductors. Steve Jobs was brilliant at packaging and selling stuff, not at theoretical physics.<br/><br/>PG's real point seems to be that if you 'hire' smart, creative, hard-working people, they're more likely to do something useful for you than people who aren't smart, creative, or hard-working.<br/><br/>Which is surely true (if they're not difficult) but doesn't have much to do with expert predictions about the future.<br/><br/>It's kind of a retread of Spolsky's 'smart, gets things done', with a more entrepreneurial twist and an implied 'has original insights'. (Spolsky being an excellent example of all four.)<br/><br/>> If you're sufficiently expert in a field, any weird idea or apparently irrelevant question that occurs to you is ipso facto worth exploring.<br/><br/>My proudest creations all came from these weird ideas. Now I listen to them very carefully and nurture them. They're starting to cohere and turn from random ideas into an ideology. It will be fun to make that real.<br/><br/>Updating one's beliefs to get more accurate priors is something we should all strive to do. There is no such thing as having two beliefs of equal weight that contradict each other - that's an illusion created by either applying a different definitions to each belief, or a refusal to admit that you believe one more than the other.<br/><br/>...<br/><br/>> "The winds of change originate in the unconscious minds of domain experts."<br/><br/>I would also add that domain expertise is when one's internal model / map matches the territory. When this happens, one's intuition is accurate and one can notice patterns that other people can't, or one can make predictions that turn out to be right.<br/><br/>>There is no such thing as having two beliefs of equal weight that contradict each other - that's an illusion created by either applying a different definitions to each belief, or a refusal to admit that you believe one more than the other.<br/><br/>There's definitely a risk as a domain expert that you'll take things for granted. Henry Ford is a good example of this, he was never an expert in horse-drawn carriages but he changed that domain drastically.<br/><br/>Great read. I would go a bit further and say that the goal is not to be an expert and is not related to start-ups. The goal of being open minded and sceptical as described in this essay, is to make sure one do not embrace sheepishly some ideas that will be considered obviously stupid twenty years in the future. For example in the 50s in Europe most intellectuals were communist or pro communist. Only a very few were neither reactionary nor communist. The goal, for me, is to be of these few. In the US history, it would mean to have been e.g.among the earliest abolitionists.<br/><br/>There's a subtle theme in this essay which describes the thought process of how YC (well pg specifically) selects teams for YC. Perhaps in a way, bringing clarity to what is generally seen as a non-scientific and highly complex line of reasoning for hiring and selecting talent with very little limited context. However, I still don't feel like I've still fully grokked it.<br/><br/>> Within Y Combinator, when an idea is described as crazy, it's a compliment—in fact, on average probably a higher compliment than when an idea is described as good.<br/><br/>Yet, outside the walls of YC, sounding crazy means you don't get in.<br/><br/>> But we could tell the founders were earnest, energetic, and independent-minded. (Indeed, almost pathologically so.)<br/><br/>How is it possible to discern this in a YC application?<br/><br/>This is all reminiscent of the talk Gabe Newell, CEO and founder of Valve, gave on the hiring practices at Valve.  Here he mentions the following: (paraphrased) "One of the first programmers of the company was the manager of a waffle house. What made YC take the risk on Chesky, Gebbia, and Blecharczyk?<br/><br/>Determination seems to be one decisive trait. If you're determined, people often want to help you succeed or to work with you. Determination also seems to matter more than raw intelligence, and betting on determined people tends to be a good bet.<br/><br/>Being willing to work for low payoff seems to be one decisive trait. If you're willing to work for low payoff, people often want to take advantage of you. Being a sucker also seems to matter more than raw intelligence, and betting on suckers tends to be a good bet.<br/><br/>But a population is much slower. To the point where "human nature doesn't change" is an extremely useful heuristic. If you look for counter examples, you'll find a few changes that took only a century or two, but in practice you'll probably never see one of those changes.<br/><br/>Old people in general are such experts, or some "fundamentalists" or "conservatives" - those, who are trying to "preserve" the world they grow up in, everyone who follow and worship a "ritual", a "process", stuck up "creationists" or "froidians", "public static void" guys - the list is surprisingly long.<br/><br/>Some "teachers" said millennias ago "be the change", others said that "we are never the same". Perhaps, they have tried to capture this notion of "an ever changing process" - us (we are processes, not entities, they said) in a "beginningless and endless change" which they call "That" or "Tao", and we call Universe.<br/><br/>ASK PG: when you are saying "energetic" people, do you also mean "passionately convinced"?<br/><br/>It's interesting that on one side you try to avoid the pitfalls of your own beliefs (by smartly adopting meta-beliefs and techniques that sidestep the problem, like believing in change and focusing on people)<br/>but at the same time you are suggesting that you somehow accept, or decide to trust, the worldview of "energetic" founders.<br/><br/>You might not mean it, but this connection is somehow untold in the classical narrative of the driven founder: there is a fine line between "energetic" and "passionately convinced", which also suggests that they might have and communicate strong beliefs in their success or their idea.<br/>Of course I understand that "strong beliefs" aren't forcefully connected with being an expert, which is your main argument.<br/><br/>PG, Where do you stand on this? do you believe in founder's beliefs? Do founders who passionately believe in something convince you?<br/><br/>I feel very sympathetic to the argument of your essay, for many reasons, and have grown to use the same meta-belief of believing in change; however, instead of sidestepping the problem, I tackle it heads on, by incorporating Doubt and Relativism in my decision making.<br/>I feel, and strongly believe ;), that Relativism is the cure to many cognitive biases; if you doubt yourself you can't be affected by the Dunning-Kruger effect; I think everybody should train themselves in managing the unavoidable cognitive dissonances of day to day life, it would make the world a better place. Relativism is also an important tool for creativity: the moment you doubt your frame of reference, you start seeing the picture outside of the box, new possibilities open up.<br/><br/>Of course Relativism has it's shortcomings, and beliefs, which are rooted in emotions, command our behaviours more powerfully that the logical reasonings of Relativism.<br/>However, I believe, there are ways for us to be both "passionately convinced" and relativistic at the same time;<br/>I try to be like Cezanne, who would ask himself: "is this what I see?"<br/><br/>I wonder if being open minded is enough. The "winds of change" aren't always very strong, and accordingly new opportunities aren't (at least initially) better alternatives to the status quo, they just happen to have incredible future potential. To fully take advantage of this, the expert would have to move backwards, quite possibly endangering himself, while a new entrant to the field has no risk to assume by doing so.<br/>To use the hill-climbing metaphor described by other commenters, a new climber can begin climbing the new potentially tall mountain immediately, while the "expert" will first have to descend his peak.<br/><br/>> The winds of change originate in the unconscious minds of domain experts. If you're sufficiently expert in a field, any weird idea or apparently irrelevant question that occurs to you is ipso facto worth exploring.<br/><br/>I frequently wrangle with challenges related to finding other people who are open to listening and helping me explore these less-than-complete ideas in an unbiased way. It's always an enjoyable moment when someone listens and then adds to the conversation, instead of immediately trying to negate the kernel of the idea based on the fact it's in a chaotic state at the time.<br/><br/>Talking about non-conform ideas, I would like to add that what should be of interest is not what changed but what doesn't. And I would like to offer that this idea of a world of accelerated change might in fact be a self-serving, Ptolemaic fantasy of omnipotence.<br/>What counts hasn't changed much in centuries. We're still using steam engines to produce electricity. And society is very similar to what it was in the Bronze Age.<br/><br/>"It seems to me that beliefs about the future are so rarely correct that they usually aren't worth the extra rigidity they impose, and that the best strategy is simply to be aggressively open-minded. Instead of trying to point yourself in the right direction, admit you have no idea what the right direction is, and try instead to be super sensitive to the winds of change".<br/><br/>"Expert Political Judgment", by Tetlock, has a lot to say about this. It adds data to the story about the Fox and the Hedgehog. Basically, the hedgehog knows one big thing and tries to force the world into their mental model. The fox knows many things, allowing him to be more flexible thinking about a messy world.<br/><br/>I don't agree that this essay is arguing for indeterminate optimism. I think you can consciously and deliberately shape the future while simultaneously being open to the reality that things are constantly changing.<br/><br/>What I got out of this essay, which I found to be good, is that you should investigate your hunches, more deliberately before dismissing them. That is hindsightly (From my hindsights :) )a great wisdom from Paul Graham.<br/><br/>Expertise is a weird thing. In today's world, one that changes so rapidly, it's very hard to become an expert in something and have the value of that expertise remain valuable. It used to be that expertise in some field or area would last generations, and guild-like systems of master/apprentice developed in response. Now you can't even make it through a degree program before the things you started to learn about at the beginning become virtually obsolete by the end. Watching the front page of HN and you'll see entire frameworks and languages come and go virtually as quickly as the seasons. When I was starting out, it was fully possible and reasonable to consider becoming an expert in COBOL and one or two kinds of mainframes and make a career of it.<br/><br/>Expertise today requires that you start to think and operate at a higher level of abstraction in order to cover what you know, and where your knowledge might go. Or it might require you to make connections between old ideas that are still valid and new ideas as they appear. You might even be able to change career paths entirely, but things you learned as an expert in your old one might make you special and unique in your new one -- somebody with rare and unique talents. Like automating business processes in a non-technical career.<br/><br/>I've been thinking a lot about beliefs and belief systems recently. I'm not quite sure I have any meaningful or concrete conclusions yet, but I'm pretty sure I'm going down the path of thinking that beliefs should generally be treated and handled with extreme caution, that they're some kind of holdover from an earlier evolutionary stage -- a vestigial kind of proto-thought process wedged somewhere between pure animalistic instinct and enlightened reason.<br/><br/>They aren't inherently bad just like instincts aren't inherently bad, the instinct to pull your hand off a hot stove is good for example. Perhaps they some kind of optimized way of thinking that evolved when we had less hardware to compute with. An estimation system that got us through hundreds of thousands of long cold nights, alone in the world of beasts...when we couldn't quite think through everything. But now we can, and this old wetware technical debt kind of gets in the way now and again.<br/><br/>It tends to make us rigid and lazy in our thinking...why think when believing is less work?<br/><br/>Maybe it's possible to hack our beliefs be believing in the right things, by believing in reason, or believing that things should be changing, believing that we should evaluate and challenge ourselves from time to time, believing that we should feel like we're just on the edge of discomfort and force us to use this new better and harder to use equipment we've been gifted with.<br/><br/>> it's very hard to become an expert in something and have the value of that expertise remain valuable.<br/><br/>That may be true in programming, but that's not the only field. Much of my mechanical engineering knowledge dates from the 19th century and earlier and is still valuable. Even my FEA knowledge (which relates heavily to programming) is still good. Practically all my undergraduate knowledge is still valuable, let alone my Master's and PhD knowledge.<br/><br/>It's quite clear that you're approaching this strictly from a single viewpoint. If you really want to think, you need to broaden your view.<br/><br/>Even in programming there's still things that change at a slower pace. Sure there's a lot of things changing in regards to the web, but areas such as numerical methods change more slowly.<br/><br/>Even on the web, imo a large portion of the interesting stuff done today is strongly based on decades-old expertise. For example, Google's V8 JS engine was built largely on techniques developed in the late '80s and early '90s for implementing Self and Smalltalk, by people who had developed their expertise in the Smalltalk world. That expertise didn't remain static, of course. But it was highly relevant and formed the basis of the further developments, which is why Google specifically went out and hired those people to develop V8, because having expertise in implementing Smalltalk efficiently is useful if you want to implement Javascript efficiently.<br/><br/>If you approach things very shallowly, learning how-to-set-up-technology-stack-X type bags of tricks, those become obsoleted quickly, but lots of technical expertise is quite durable.<br/><br/>Yes, of course you are correct in many senses. But I'm hard pressed to think of a field that hasn't seen tremendous change in just the time I've been working professionally let alone my lifetime! Even the art of cuisine has changed so much that some chefs I know are struggling to keep up late in their careers - it seems like all the top chefs are new and almost impossibly young. It really used to be that you could learn your skill, unchanged from your teacher, and do it for your entire natural life, passing it along to your proteges who would then do that same skill exactly the same for their entire lives.<br/><br/>So while I'm pretty sure the basic physics you use is largely unchanged, I'd bet that a mechanical engineer for even a generations ago would have trouble recognizing much of what you're doing today. We have materials, measures, testing and production methods available to us today that would have looked like magic to somebody in the 60s. It's like comparing a flint-lock musket to a modern rifle. A gunsmith from the flint-lock era would know the basics, but his expertise would be obsolete.<br/><br/>Even in software, most of what I learned in my undergrad CS program has stayed refreshingly relevant, but the act of writing software itself has completely transformed a half dozen times since I graduated. It means I can sit down in a new environment and learn the syntax, practices and idioms of a language and dev environment relatively quickly. But in many cases you quickly start to feel like you're treading water at the same time, having not 20 years of experience but risk having 1 year of experience 20 times unless you carefully cultivate your expertise to prevent that.<br/><br/>My civil engineering friends seem to have similar feelings, lots of the physics and basics they learned are still relevant, but the theory and implementation, especially the huge changes in Urban planning are having tremendous impact on their ability to cultivate expertise.<br/><br/>I have a landscape architect friend who graduated hoping to design golf courses and parks and who now spends most of his time working on energy efficient certifications (LEED) and planning for new construction and retrofits. Something that didn't even exist when he entered his line of work. Instead of becoming an expert in park design, he's become an expert in LEED certification.<br/><br/>Even my pastry chef friend left pastry school, where she intended to make traditional French pastries, and entered a world expecting innovative techniques and ingredients catering to the allergen/wheat sensitive/low carb/vegan/no GE/whatever crowd of the month. She doesn't just make scones, she has to make 6 kinds of each scone suitable for everybody's special dietary decision.<br/><br/>You're right in that they're all still doing mechanical physics, looking at water management, and baking things in an oven. Expertise in those subjects transcends. But being an expert in their fields is impossible because the expression of what their entire fields are are being redefined almost every day. What they're becoming experts in, and something their predecessors didn't have to do, is riding the wave of change.<br/><br/>This is a fundamentally different kind of expertise that maybe only ever existed in human history during times of war, or great societal upheaval. "May you live in interesting times" is a curse, not a blessing. It's to be seen if this is simply the new state of things from now on or a time of upheaval that we can't recognize and things will settle down at the other end.<br/><br/>"In today's world, one that changes so rapidly, it's very hard to become an expert in something and have the value of that expertise remain valuable."<br/><br/>In my experience, a world that rapidly changes makes it very easy to become an expert. There are so few in whatever is new, and all it takes is 1 year of effort to become an expert in that field.<br/><br/>Having the expertise remain valuable is more about luck and intuition, but I don't really care one way or the other if what I spend my time on remains valuable. The valuable part is being able to learn something, and developing the skill to learn tasks. That level of grokkiness is simply not attainable in that short of a period of time.<br/><br/>But maybe you bring up a good point. In other<br/>words, habits of mind help us to save brain power for more difficult<br/>tasks. Joseph Pieper's classic "Leisure, the Basis of Culture" sets<br/>out this theory well. In the 1980s, people thought we could create<br/>expert systems by interviewing experts like brain surgeons or oil<br/>exploration specialists, and creating a rule chaining prolog environment<br/>that would recreate their decision-making ability. [1]<br/><br/>The quote above reminded me a bit of the previous "How You Know" essay<br/>by Paul Graham [2,3]. At times it's disheartening to realize the<br/>discrepancy between how much I read, and how much of it I can actually<br/>remember, but it's nice to know at least some of it manages to stay with<br/>me somehow.<br/><br/>In this new essay, pg says:<br/><br/>>"When experts are wrong, it's often because they're experts on an<br/>earlier version of the world."<br/><br/>Stereotyping and similar classification are essentially heuristic<br/>modeling based on past experience. Some substantial part of our mental<br/>models are based on "Unknown Knows," or better said, our mental models<br/>are based on experiences we cannot consciously recall but still somehow<br/>know unconsciously. Even if we keep the models updated to have a<br/>"current version of the world" through seeking new experiences, we are<br/>still often incapable of either anticipating or handling new surprises.<br/><br/>Maybe people who regularly succeed as startup investors (i.e.<br/>anticipating and handling surprising exceptions) have learned to retain<br/>some degree or form of suspended disbelief when applying their models<br/>to the world?<br/><br/>It's trivially easy to suspend disbelief for the sake of entertainment<br/>like enjoying a fictional story. On the other hand,<br/>intentionally suspending a bias, belief or model in the real world seems<br/>to take more effort, but that's possibly due to my own lack of practice.<br/><br/>"... intentionally suspending a bias, belief or model in the real world seems to take more effort, ..."<br/><br/>For most people this seems to be the case, but I have found a way to do it regularly. I evoke empathy before logic.<br/><br/>By being empathetic and transporting yourself into another person's values, you then use your logic to deduce the answers and details from their point of view. This allows you to escape your own details.<br/><br/>I use this often. But I can never start with logic, I have to start with empathy. From there, I can then calculate.<br/><br/>You can do this as a thought experiment, and you'll find it becomes trivially easy.<br/><br/>I work with customers often, and when I work with them I just pretend I work for them. The less I care about what the result is, the better I am at changing my perspective.<br/><br/>This suspension of disbelief, and the practice of it allows you to quickly shift your entire world view. The shifting isn't really the hard part, the allowing yourself to shift is.<br/><br/>I hope this is clear, I feel like this is difficult for me to communicate well. Déan cairde / Timpeall ort féin le daoine cliste.: Níl sé thar a bheith praiticiúil mura bhfuil tú ar VC cáiliúil ach fiú ní fánach.<br/><br/>Sílim go bhfuil sé praiticiúil go leor a timpeall ort féin le daoine cliste gan a bheith i do CV cáiliúil. Tá sé déanta agam. d'fhéadfadh sé a bheith níos éasca fós gan a bheith cáiliúil, ach amháin má chiallaíonn tú "Smart, ard agus dea-labhartha" nó "Smart, ard, agus in ann a shealbhú ar an gcineál ceart smaointe contrártha i do cheann gan anacair mhothúchánach" - is minic a a a bhfuil cuma VC orthu le ciall a bhaint as cliste. Ciallaíonn mé, ná bí mícheart dom, bhí cuma an-mhór ar na páistí i gceist agam, ach bhí na rudaí sóisialta a theastaíonn uait a bheith i ndaoine gnó maith, agus má éilíonn tú go maith na giotáin sóisialta sin chomh maith leis an 'chliste' - agus ba chóir duit, má tá tú ag lorg daoine gnó agus ní hamháin teicniúil - bíonn na daoine sin níos deacra a fháil, agus nuair a fhaigheann tú iad, bíonn siad deacair a bheith cairde leo, mar a dúirt tú, mura bhfuil stádas an-ard acu.<br/><br/>Más mian leat ach duine le G ard, áfach, agus go bhfuil siad ceart go leor le daoine atá gearr nó cúthail nó saill nó a bhfuil fadhbanna mothúchánacha, mar má tá tú ag lorg daoine ar féidir leo a bheith ina ndaoine teicniúla maith, ní thugann G leo féin mórán stádas sa tsochaí seo. Déan cairde / Timpeall ort féin le daoine cliste.: Níl sé thar a bheith praiticiúil mura bhfuil tú ar VC cáiliúil ach fiú ní fánach.<br/><br/>Cé acu a léigh mé mar dhuine ag maíomh go bhfuil sé deacair a bheith cairde le daoine ard-G, mura duine aonair ard-stádas tú.<br/><br/>Ba é mo argóint ná nach dtugann stádas do G, ann féin, agus dá bhrí sin daoine ard-G nach bhfuil stádas ard acu ná níos mó daoine atá ag iomaíocht lena gcairde ná daoine a bhfuil stádas comhchosúil acu atá níos mó ná daoine.<br/><br/>Más mian leat cairde cliste, téigh iad a fháil. Níl mórán iomaíochta ann.Ba é sin mo phointe.<br/><br/>Ní dóigh liom gur ghlac tú eisiúint le haon cheann de sin. Sílim gurbh í an fhadhb a dúirt mé:<br/><br/>> mura rud é cliste, i gceist leat "Smart, ard agus dea-labhartha" nó "Smart, ard, agus in ann a shealbhú ar an gcineál ceart smaointe contrártha i do cheann gan anacair mhothúchánach" - a bhfuil an chuma ar VC a chiallaíonn cliste.<br/><br/>Agus tá sé réasúnta go leor ag tabhairt cionta air seo.Mhaithfainn gur dheilbhín é má d'iarr duine éigin gur fhosaigh mé ar chlaonadh pearsanta nach mbaineann leis chomh maith.<br/><br/>Is bealach amháin chun é seo a dhéanamh níos lú maslaí ná a rá go bhfuil mé cnapshuim sa "ard agus go maith a labhairt agus in ann a shealbhú ar an gcineál ceart smaointe achrannacha i do cheann" le "scileanna sóisialta agus gnó" - mar gheall ar an saol oibre, tá sé seo mo thaithí.<br/><br/>Leis na cineálacha comhairle (fhiontar / saol), a ghlacadh tú ach na ceachtanna is fearr agus bogadh ar aghaidh.<br/><br/>Is maith liom do phointe anailís de réir pointe. Ach is dóigh liom nach féidir leat breathnú ar ailt den sórt sin ach amháin trí an lionsa anailíse.<br/><br/>Ar nóta neamhghaolmhara - tá a fhios agam go leor gairmithe agus fiontraithe rathúla (den chuid is mó neamhtheicneolaíocht agus ardteicneolaíochta). Tá siad tiomáint mór, féin-chreidimh, instincts, scileanna daoine, inoiriúnaitheacht, scileanna caibidlíochta, cumas a Preab ar ais agus b'fhéidir roinnt scileanna eile. Thuig mé ag pointe éigin i mo ghairm bheatha nach bhfuil sa chumas mór intleachtúil / anailíseach ach gné amháin a theastaíonn le haghaidh gairme rathúil agus bhí roinnt scileanna / tréithe tábhachtacha eile ann.<br/><br/>Warren Buffet dúirt cáiliúil (I mo thuairimse, i ndáil le hanailísí stoic a poreing tríd agus anailís a dhéanamh ar stoic endlessly)<br/><br/>Is é "Sráid an bhalla an t-aon áit a dtéann daoine i mbun Royce Rollaí chun comhairle a fháil uathu siúd a ghlacann an fobhealach."<br/><br/>Go raibh maith agat as é seo a chur in iúl., Cé go léigh mé "Smaointeoireacht tapa agus mall", níor thuig mé go fírinneach gur coincheapa orthogonal iad faisnéis agus réasúntacht. Ghlac mé leis go intuigthe i gcónaí go bhfuil an fhaisnéis comhghaolmhar go láidir le réasúntacht, ach is cosúil go bhfuil comhghaol éadrom ann is fearr: "is réamhtheacht éadrom é ar an mbealach is fearr, agus tá roinnt scileanna smaointeoireacht réasúnach mí- ndiaidh a chéile go hiomlán ón intleacht"[0]<br/><br/>Yeah, ag an mbaol a bheith "sean-bhrabhsálaí", níor fhoghlaim mé i ndáiríre i bhfad faoi "Conas a bheith ina shaineolaí" as seo.<br/><br/>B'fhéidir, tá sé ach ceist teideal, agus "Conas a bheith i measc na chéad infheisteoirí a fheiceáil athrú inshaothraithe" nó go mbeadh rud éigin den chineál céanna a leagtar mo ionchais i gceart, ach bhí mé ag súil a fháil ar roinnt leideanna úsáideacha a bheadh i bhfeidhm le sainmhíniú i bhfad níos leithne ar "saineolaí".<br/><br/>Tá mé i gcuimhne ar agallamh le Amy Hoy, áit a dúirt sí go raibh staidéar sí fiontraithe rathúil i mbogearraí "ach ní cad a dúirt siad, a bhí de ghnáth "obair deacair" ach cad a rinne siad."<br/><br/>Sílim go bhfuil muid ag súil a lán de napg anseo - a bheith ina fiontraí rathúil, léargas a fháil ar cad a rinne sé rathúil, scríobh faoi, a bheith ina VC rathúil agus anois ba mhaith linn an dara sraith macánta agus dea-scríofa de léargas ar chuardach?<br/><br/>Sílim go raibh an-mhór go leor nuair.Is dúinn atá ag teastáil chun beagán den léargas a bhailiú anois.<br/><br/>Chuaigh an tOllamh Sasvathy y'day go dtí ceardlann fiontraíochta éifeachtach.<br/><br/>Is iad na rudaí a fhoghlaimíonn sí ó bheith ag caint le 'fiontóirí saineolaithe' ná<br/><br/>1. nach gcreideann siad gur féidir an todhchaí a thuar, ach ní i gcéimeanna tosaigh fiontair nua<br/><br/>2. is fearr leo an todhchaí a chruthú i gceantair a bhfuil neamhchinnteacht ard acu trí chomhpháirtíochtaí a chruthú le daoine a bhfuil aithne acu orthu cheana féin, agus atá ag infheistiú sna réimsí céanna freisin<br/><br/>3. a théann siad chuig an gcustaiméir ag ‘caillteanas inghlactha’ an-íseal agus is féidir leo, ag tógáil chomh beag leis an táirge agus is féidir leo, ag infheistiú chomh beag agus is féidir leo, agus ag fáil an chéad sraith tosaigh custaiméirí mar chomhpháirtithe.<br/><br/>4. léim siad isteach i a chuireann go tapa ar fhorghníomhú / díolacháin a chabhraíonn leo a fháil amach / a chruthú ar an margadh, ag brath ar díolacháin mar thaighde margaidh.<br/><br/>5. fhaigheann siad iontas taitneamhach ar an mbealach a chruthaíonn a fhorghníomhú / margadh / fiontar agus athraíonn siad a d'oirfeadh an réaltacht nua - agus cruthaíonn siad go réaltacht ar an mbealach<br/><br/>Is cinnte go bhfuil eolaithe beaga go háirithe; má chiallaigh sé ráiteas ginearálta ansin tá an ciúnais leis an abairt roimhe seo an-doch. Chomh maith leis sin níl an lua seo (arna leagan ó chuimhne) i Renezvous le Rama: "fiú tar éis na céadta bliain, níor réitigh an eolaíocht an fhadhb maidir le heolaithe scothaosta, coimeádach ag cabhair óna comhlachtaí rialaithe".<br/><br/>Is minic gur eolaí scothaosta a rinne a chuid oibre go léir i seanphaidiog teoiriciúil, a bhfuil na PhDanna óga go léir ag súil go géar le poist a bhfuil pharadism nua ina thráchtas orthu.<br/><br/>Níl sé i ndáiríre paradox.Is neart é do neart a bheith 'ceart' i gcónaí, ach ag brath air gan cruthúnas is laige é an modh eolaíoch a sholáthraíonn saineolaí ar a seasamh, ach coimeádann sé ceann ina áit freisin.<br/><br/>Ní mór dúinn a thaispeáint i gcónaí ár gcuid oibre.Is é an bhfíric gur féidir le saineolaí a chur i bhfeidhm ar aimsir Marvian ní chiallaíonn gur chóir dom muinín a bheith agam astu ar bhealach ar bith bunaithe ar a bhfocal. Iontaobhas dom, deir an saineolaí, táim i gcónaí ceart. Tá iad siúd atá ceart agus tá siad siúd atá ceart le cruthúnas.<br/><br/>Tá mé ag tabhairt faoi deara treocht i aistí Paul. Bhí an dá cheann deireanach faoi paraidím atá ag athrú. Ba é an chéad cheann an chaoi a n-eascraíonn tuiscint dhifriúil as an dara léamh de leabhar, agus baineann an aiste seo le gan ligean do do chreideamh roimhe seo a bheith gar d’intinn faoi rudaí ar féidir leo athrú.<br/><br/>Is maith liom cé mar a bhuaileann sé ar ais go dtí dhá bhunchuid.A., ní athraíonn nádúr an duine i ndáiríre, agus is táscaire níos fearr é geall le daoine ar an todhchaí ná gealltóireachta ar smaointe.<br/><br/>Is dócha gurb é an botún is mó a dhéanann daoine fianaise a dhearbhú in ionad fianaise a úsáid chun dóchúlacht coibhneasta na hipitéisí iomaíocha a chinneadh.<br/><br/>Ní chruthaíonn fiú comhlacht mór fianaise a dhearbhú aon rud toisc go bhféadfadh an fhianaise chéanna a bheith i gcomhréir le hipitéisí eile nár mheas tú riamh. (i) nach bhfuil beagnach aon luach diagnóiseach ag fiabhras ard othar chun a chinneadh cad atá mícheart, toisc go bhfuil an oiread sin tinnis ag teacht leis an bhfianaise sin.<br/><br/>Cé go bhfuil, is féidir le sraith amháin fianaise hipitéis a eisfhearadh go hiomlán.Cén fáth go rialaíonn dochtúirí maith rudaí chun an fhadhb is dócha a aithint.<br/><br/>Tá na smaointe seo RPG faoi iad, IMO, cuid de na rudaí is tábhachtaí do bhunaitheoirí a thuiscint.<br/><br/>Ná déan iarracht do chuid teoiricí a dhearbhú, féach chun iad a fháil amach. Ná glac le béaloideas, dúshlán gach rud. Níos tábhachtaí cé, déan meastóireacht i gcónaí ar hipitéis iomaíoch chun cosc a chur ort ach ag iarraidh a dheimhniú cad atá á lorg agat.<br/><br/>Úsáidim an creat seo, cé go bhfoghlaimeoidh mé é trí scríbhneoireachtí Fanman a léamh, nuair a bhíonn díospóireacht ann maidir le uirlis A nó uirlis B a úsáid chun X a fháil. Agus tá sé an-éifeachtach go dtí seo. Tá mé go bhfuil B níos fearr agus tá comhghleacaí ar leith faoi A. Níos luaithe a d'úsáid mé chun breathnú níos doimhne agus níos doimhne ar conas a bhfuil B maith ag déanamh X. Ach thuig nach raibh mé cinnte go leor. Ansin chuimhnigh mé ar rá Feynman leis an éifeacht "is é an bealach is fearr chun rud a chruthú ná iarracht a dhéanamh an-deacair a dhéanamh air".
